topics.headline: California Legislature just put $5B more on the table for K-12 than Newsom proposed — including a $5B no-strings block grant for charters
topics.category: A (state funding) — strongest topic from the `policy_funding_hawk` lens
topics.why_matters: Every Tier A partner — Visions, Heritage Peak, Sage Oak, iLEAD, Heartland, Compass, all of them — is about to receive (a) a higher COLA, (b) more special ed funding, and (c) a discretionary block grant they can legally point at intervention contracts. The LAO explicitly counts charter schools as "districts" for Prop 98 distribution purposes. The negotiation window between the Legislature and Newsom is the next two weeks. This is the single biggest "what to spend it on" conversation charter directors will have all summer, and A+ has a credible answer for the SpEd and discretionary buckets.
topics.angle: "California charter schools are about to get a $5B one-time block grant they can spend however they want. Here are the three things our partner schools are already telling us they're prioritizing."
topics.roman_take: The discretionary block grant is a trap for any charter without a measurement framework — money you can spend "however you want" gets spent on things no one can defend at the next board meeting. Tie discretionary dollars to ESSA-evidenced interventions or watch them disappear into the general fund.
topics.danielle_take: Concrete checklist for charter business managers: how to code the block grant against existing intervention contracts, what to ask your CFO before October 1, and which partner schools have already started this conversation. ---

topics.headline: iLEAD Schools launches new Lancaster charter (iLEAD Flex) as California's NCB moratorium ends
topics.category: C (Tier A partner news) — primary lens hit
topics.why_matters: iLEAD is a Tier A partner across multiple campuses (Antelope Valley, Lancaster Charter Academy, Exploration, Hybrid, Online). iLEAD Schools announced the expansion of its charter network into Lancaster, California, adding a new charter school in their network beginning in 2026. The unlock is structural: since the statewide moratorium on new nonclassroom-based charters concluded on January 1, 2026, mission-driven networks like iLEAD have been moving quickly to bring their programs to communities that have long lacked access to flex learning. iLEAD Flex will open in August 2026 — meaning an intervention staffing/program-build window opens in the next 60 days. The existing iLEAD Lancaster Charter context makes the intervention case sharp: In Ilead Lancaster Charter District, 17% of students tested at or above the proficient level for reading, and 10% tested at or above that level for math.
topics.angle: "California's NCB moratorium ended six months ago. The first wave of partner expansions is starting — here's what that means for intervention planning."
topics.roman_take: Conviction angle on why the moratorium lift is a generational opening for flex-based public schools — and why intervention infrastructure (not just enrollment marketing) determines which new charters survive renewal.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation angle on the 60-day window between charter approval and August opening — what Title III / Title I funding decisions need to be made now so a new charter doesn't start year one with a reactive intervention model. ---

topics.headline: The Nashville Problem — why big tutoring vendors keep failing at scale, and what charter directors should ask before signing
topics.why_matters: The field's most credible institutions are now openly admitting that vendor-led high-dosage tutoring stops working when it scales — impacts decline as programs scale up, and some of the most effective programs are intensive, expensive, and difficult to implement. That's exactly the A+ thesis stated by Harvard and AIBM. The Harvard toolkit launch reframes "evidence-based tutoring" from a marketing line into a procurement standard: "Measure, Monitor, Improve" aims to help solve common challenges when bringing tutoring programs to students and provides a set of data collection strategies, tools, and best practices for district leaders, policymakers, and academic researchers. A+ should plant a flag here before Varsity, IXL, and Saga rewrite the narrative.
topics.angle: "Harvard just told K-12 leaders the quiet part out loud: tutoring works in studies and fails at scale. Here's what charter directors should ask any vendor before signing — including us."
topics.roman_take: Conviction angle — the reason small operators outperform "platform" tutoring at scale isn't capacity, it's that the platforms sell hours and we sell outcomes; Nashville bought 125,000 hours and got minimal gains because hours aren't the unit of impact.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation angle — walk through the four conditions Harvard says actually drive results (it happens during the school day, districts that include tutoring into existing learning blocks saw far higher participation than tutoring outside of core hours, it's measured, data enables visibility of student participation and monitoring of tutoring frequency and duration, it depends on people, proper training of supported and reliable tutors gets the best results, and programs are held accountable) and show how A+'s iEM program is already structured exactly that way at Ocean Grove + South Sutter + Sky Mountain. ---
